ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for double-layer steamed milk custard (shuangpi nai). FOODON_03301710 (milk-based dessert food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Milk-based desserts) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for shuangpi nai or osmanthus-flavoured steamed milk custard.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Chinese steamed milk custard preparation.

main_ingredients: fermented glutinous rice (jiuniang / rice wine lees)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one individual bowl or cup as typically served in a Cantonese dessert shop (tong sui pou).

nutrient_source.title: Shuangpi Nai (Double-Skin Milk Custard) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component ingredients: whole milk (FDC 746782), egg white (FDC 172183), sugar (FDC 169655), cooked lotus seeds (FDC 169998), and osmanthus/fermented rice contribution estimated from analogous flavouring agents
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (component-based estimation)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for osmanthus double-layer milk custard with lotus seed exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted recipe model: approximately 70% whole milk, 10% egg white, 8% sugar, 8% cooked lotus seeds, 4% fermented glutinous rice and osmanthus flavouring. The recipe model is consistent with published Cantonese shuangpi nai formulations. Values are rounded to one decimal place. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk-based desserts and dairy foods — analogous estimate based on whole milk (GI ~27, University of Sydney GI Database) and added sugar contribution; composite GI estimated at ~40 for sweetened steamed milk custard
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for osmanthus double-layer milk custard with lotus seed. GI is estimated at 40 (low category) based on: (1) whole milk GI of approximately 27 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); (2) added sucrose contribution raising composite GI; (3) lotus seeds having a low-to-medium GI (~40–50, analogous to other legume seeds per University of Sydney GI Database); (4) fermented rice contribution is minor. Composite GI of 40 is a conservative low-category estimate. Glycemic load per serving = (40 × 15.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 12.2 g (using 200 g serving and 15.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~48 mg per 100 g; ~96 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: The sodium content of approximately 96 mg per standard 200 g serving is well within the recommended limit of 2300 mg per day, making this dessert acceptable for individuals with hypertension when consumed in moderation as an occasional treat.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (~12.8 g sugar per 100 g; ~25.6 g per serving) and estimated low GI of 40
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the estimated glycemic index is low (~40), the total sugar load of approximately 25.6 g per 200 g serving is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control is advised and this dessert should be consumed infrequently as part of a carbohydrate-managed meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g; ~4.2 g per serving) from whole milk; low trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of approximately 4.2 g per 200 g serving is moderate; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings and consider the cumulative saturated fat intake from other dietary sources across the day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Osmanthus Double-Layer Milk Custard with Lotus Seed (桂花双皮奶配莲子; Guìhuā Shuāngpí Nǎi pèi Liánzǐ) is a refined Cantonese steamed milk dessert originating from Guangdong province, China, and widely served in Cantonese dessert shops (tong sui pou / 糖水铺) across Singapore and Hong Kong. The dish is a variation of the classic shuangpi nai (double-skin milk), which is made by heating fresh whole milk until a skin forms, then carefully inverting the milk so a second skin forms beneath the first, creating a silky double-layered custard texture. In this version, the custard is flavoured with osmanthus flowers (桂花, guìhuā) — either dried flowers or osmanthus-infused syrup — which impart a delicate floral sweetness and golden colour. Fermented glutinous rice (醪糟 / 酒酿, jiuniang) is incorporated to add a subtle fermented sweetness and aromatic complexity. Premium Hunan lotus seeds (莲子, liánzǐ) are added as a topping or stirred in, contributing a mild, slightly starchy flavour and a soft, mealy texture. The dessert is steamed in individual portions, resulting in a smooth, silken, panna-cotta-like consistency. Nutritionally, a 200 g serving provides approximately 216 kcal, 8.4 g protein, 7.0 g fat, and 30.4 g carbohydrate, with a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 40. The calcium content (~240 mg per serving) contributes meaningfully to daily calcium requirements. The dessert contains milk and egg allergens and is not suitable for vegans or those avoiding alcohol (fermented rice wine). Clinically, the moderate sugar content (~25.6 g per serving) warrants portion awareness for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The low sodium content makes it suitable for most individuals with hypertension. This dessert is culturally associated with traditional Chinese herbal and sweet soup cuisine and is commonly consumed as an afternoon or post-dinner treat.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for osmanthus double-layer milk custard with lotus seed was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model using USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries (whole milk, egg white, sugar, cooked lotus seeds). GI is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database values for whole milk and comparable dairy-based desserts. Confidence is medium due to the estimation basis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a recipe-component model (USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries) as no direct database entry exists for this specific dish. The recipe model assumes approximately 70% whole milk, 10% egg white, 8% sugar, 8% cooked lotus seeds, and 4% fermented glutinous rice and osmanthus flavouring by weight. 2. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Beverages'; this has been corrected to 'Desserts' as osmanthus double-layer milk custard is a steamed solid/semi-solid dessert, not a beverage. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; this is confirmed as effectively zero for a fresh milk and egg white preparation with no hydrogenated fats — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 4. The product description notes fermented glutinous rice wine (jiuniang); this means the product is NOT halal-certified and is_halal is set to false. 5. GI is an analogous estimate of 40 (low category) based on University of Sydney GI Database values for whole milk (GI ~27) and the composite effect of added sugar and lotus seeds. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all micronutrients are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and carry medium confidence. 7. Sentinel value -1 is NOT used in this record; all fields have been estimated from component data or set to 0.0 where confirmed negligible. 8. The osmanthus flowers and fermented rice contribute negligible macronutrients at the quantities used (~4% combined) and their micronutrient contribution is subsumed within the estimation uncertainty. 9. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_100g × serving_size_g) / 100 = (40 × 15.2 × 200) / 10000 = 12.16, rounded to 12.2.
